data.body:
=== RESEARCH REPORT ===
=== prior-art: agile DoR / INVEST / story-splitting / LLM-as-judge / RFC prior-art
=== agent a92b1fca9a73a16b8

All verification checks pass cleanly. Every load-bearing claim I spot-checked — across all six research areas — was confirmed verbatim against primary sources. One refinement: the Humanizing Work guide has two authors (Richard Lawrence and Peter Green), not Lawrence alone. With verification complete, here is the synthesized report.

---

# Research Report: Prior Art for an Automated Ticket-Readiness / Plan-Verification Gate

Scope: published practice and research relevant to an LLM-driven gate that runs when a work ticket moves open → in-progress, to catch oversized/ambiguous work, inaccurate file references, plans that ignore consumers or tests, unvalidated novel architecture, AC/description mismatch, and incomplete child coverage. All key claims (arXiv IDs, attributions, thresholds, template wording) were adversarially verified against primary sources; verification notes are flagged inline.

---

## 1. Definition of Ready (DoR)

**Key concept.** DoR is a team's explicit, shared set of entry criteria a backlog item must meet *before* it is pulled into a sprint and started — the entry-gate analog of the Definition of Done's exit gate. Crucially, **DoR is not in the Scrum Guide** (DoD is a formal Scrum commitment; DoR is an optional complementary practice), and it is conventionally tied to the INVEST heuristic.

**DoR vs DoD (the clean framing):** "The Definition of Ready covers the requirements *coming into* the sprint; the Definition of Done covers the product *coming out of* the sprint." DoR applies to individual backlog items; DoD applies to the Increment.

**Reusable DoR checklist** (consolidated from Atlassian, Scrum Alliance, Agile Alliance, Mountain Goat). A story is Ready when: user story is clear/well-formed; business value is articulated; **acceptance criteria are defined, measurable, and testable**; the team shares understanding of it; it is small enough to fit one sprint; it is estimated; dependencies are identified and resolved; there are no blockers; UX/design is attached where applicable; INVEST is satisfied; stakeholders are aligned.

**Important controversy (directly relevant to a *gate*).** DoR is widely criticized as an anti-pattern *when it hardens into a mandatory approval gate*. Mike Cohn warns a strict DoR becomes "a huge step towards a sequential, stage-gate approach"; Scrum Alliance says it can "place documentation, processes, and sticking to a plan over individuals, interactions, and collaboration — the inverse of the Agile Manifesto." The recurring recommendation: keep DoR as a **flexible guideline / conversation prompt**, not a hard pass/fail bar. For your design, this argues for a gate that *advises and surfaces* problems (and is overridable) rather than one that hard-blocks — mirroring rebar's existing `--force-close` escape hatch.

**Sources:**
- Mike Cohn / Mountain Goat — "The Definition of Ready and Its Dangers" — https://www.mountaingoatsoftware.com/blog/the-dangers-of-a-definition-of-ready
- Agile Alliance glossary — "Definition of Ready" — https://agilealliance.org/glossary/definition-of-ready/ (defines DoR as criteria "generally based on the INVEST matrix")
- Scrum Alliance — "Pros and Cons of a Definition of Ready" — https://resources.scrumalliance.org/Article/pros-cons-definition-ready
- Scrum.org — "Difference Between the DoD and the DoR" — https://www.scrum.org/resources/blog/what-difference-between-definition-done-dod-and-definition-ready-dor
- Atlassian — "Definition of Ready" — https://www.atlassian.com/agile/project-management/definition-of-ready

---

## 2. INVEST (and analogs for tasks/bugs/epics)

**Origin (verified).** Bill Wake, "INVEST in Good Stories, and SMART Tasks," XP123, **August 17, 2003** — https://xp123.com/invest-in-good-stories-and-smart-tasks/. Popularized by Mike Cohn's *User Stories Applied* (2004).

**The six (verified verbatim):** **I**ndependent, **N**egotiable, **V**aluable (Agile Alliance phrases it "Valuable *or vertical*" — a vertical slice), **E**stimable, **S**mall ("so as to fit within an iteration"), **T**estable ("in principle, even if there isn't a test yet").

**Operationalizing "Small" / too-big (automatable signals):**
- Story-point ceiling: a common rule is split anything **> 8 points** before sprint planning; Atlassian cites **~16 hours / ~20 points** as "too hard to estimate confidently → break down."
- The **6–10-stories-per-sprint** rule of thumb (Humanizing Work): if you can't fit 6–10 into a sprint, stories are too big.
- Can't fit in one sprint = the canonical too-big signal; that is also the definition of an epic.
- Structural/linguistic: conjunctions ("and"/"or"), the verb **"manage"** (and other CRUD-umbrella verbs), many AC items spanning different concerns, multiple personas, multiple workflow steps.

**Analogs for other types:**
- **Tasks → SMART** (Wake's own analog, same 2003 article, verified): **S**pecific, **M**easurable, **A**chievable, **R**elevant, **T**ime-boxed.
- **Bugs → "ready" field set** (industry consensus, no single acronym): title, **steps to reproduce**, **expected vs actual**, environment, severity/impact, visual evidence. This maps directly onto rebar's bug template (`## Reproduction Steps` + Expected vs Actual).
- **Epics → well-formed criteria:** clear narrative, high-level AC + scope boundary, a **measurable success metric** (outcome-focused, not technical spec), too big for one sprint by definition, decomposes into ~3–10 stories. Maps onto rebar's `## Success Criteria` / `## Context`.

**Critiques worth knowing.** Wake himself called **Estimable** "the most-abused aspect of INVEST (most energy for least value)" and later suggested swapping E=Estimable → **E=External**. **Independent** is an ideal, not a gate. Meta-critique: INVEST gets weaponized as a "bouncer" that blocks work — again arguing against a rigid gate.

**Sources:**
- Bill Wake — https://xp123.com/invest-in-good-stories-and-smart-tasks/
- Agile Alliance — "INVEST" — https://agilealliance.org/glossary/invest/
- Maarten Dalmijn — "Revisiting INVEST 20+ Years Later" — https://mdalmijn.com/p/revisiting-invest-20-years-later
- Atlassian — "Epics, Stories, Themes" — https://www.atlassian.com/agile/project-management/epics-stories-themes

---

## 3. Story splitting / decomposition

**SPIDR (Mike Cohn) — verified.** "Almost every story can be split with one of five techniques": **S**pike (time-boxed research for unknowns; last resort), **P**ath (multiple ways to accomplish the goal), **I**nterface (split by platform/device, or simple-UI-first), **D**ata (subset of data first), **R**ules (temporarily relax business rules). — https://www.mountaingoatsoftware.com/blog/five-simple-but-powerful-ways-to-split-user-stories

**Richard Lawrence & Peter Green — "How to Split a User Story" (Humanizing Work) — verified.** A 3-step flowchart (confirm it's a valuable story meeting INVEST except "Small" → apply a pattern → evaluate splits, preferring those that reveal deprioritizable work and yield comparable sizes) plus **9 patterns**: Workflow Steps, Business Rule Variations, Major Effort, Simple/Complex, Variations in Data, Data Entry Methods, Deferred Performance, **Operations (CRUD)** — where "the word 'manage' is a giveaway that the story covers multiple operations" — and Break Out a Spike. Includes the verified **6–10 stories per sprint** size rule. (Note: authored by Lawrence *and* Green, not Lawrence alone.) — https://www.humanizingwork.com/the-humanizing-work-guide-to-splitting-user-stories/ · Flowchart PDF: https://www.humanizingwork.com/wp-content/uploads/2020/10/HW-Story-Splitting-Flowchart.pdf

**Vertical vs horizontal slicing.** Vertical = thin end-to-end cut delivering user-visible value (recommended; preserves INVEST "Valuable"). Horizontal = split by technical layer (anti-pattern; no slice delivers value alone). Core principle: every slice must independently deliver end-to-end value.

**Directly automatable "too big" signals (the most reusable part for your gate):**

| Signal | Detection | Strength |
|---|---|---|
| Conjunctions "and/or/but/with" in title/narrative | regex / token scan — "first thing to do with a big story" | Strong |
| Too many acceptance criteria | count `- [ ]` items vs threshold (cited ranges: ideally 1–3 / "<5"; ">12 too large") | Strong |
| CRUD / "manage" / "configure" verbs | keyword set {manage, configure, administer, create, read, update, delete, search} | Strong (Lawrence) |
| Multiple personas | count `As a <role>` clauses; >1 → candidate | Medium |
| Multiple workflow steps | sequence words (then/after/first…next) | Medium |
| Multiple data types/channels | lists of variants (web/mobile/API, iOS/Android) | Medium |
| Estimate ≥ threshold | story points ≥ cap (Atlassian: ~20 pts) | Strong (needs metadata) |

**Sources:** Cohn SPIDR (above); Humanizing Work (above); Atlassian — "Decomposing user stories in Jira" — https://www.atlassian.com/blog/development/break-decomposing-user-stories-jira (too-big signals: "sounds like a feature," can't fit a sprint, "20 story points or more").

---

## 4. LLM-as-judge & agent-planning verification

**Seminal LLM-as-judge — verified.** Zheng et al., "Judging LLM-as-a-Judge with MT-Bench and Chatbot Arena," **arXiv:2306.05685** (NeurIPS 2023). GPT-4 judges **match human preferences at >80% agreement** (≈ human-human agreement). Three named biases (the canonical taxonomy): **position**, **verbosity**, **self-enhancement** (plus weak math/logic grading). Three reusable judging modes: **pairwise comparison**, **single-answer grading**, **reference-guided grading**. — https://arxiv.org/abs/2306.05685

**Grading issues/PRs/specs with LLMs:**
- **microsoft/LLM-RUBRIC** (ACL 2024) — multidimensional, *calibrated* rubric; the LLM is asked **one rubric question at a time** and outputs a distribution, then a calibration net maps to human ratings. The most directly reusable "rubric judge" reference. — https://github.com/microsoft/LLM-Rubric
- **Sphinx** — checklist-based benchmark for LLM-driven PR review, scoring coverage of structured verification points — https://arxiv.org/pdf/2601.04252
- **GitHub blog** — review agent PRs by **separating the analysis step from execution with an approval gate** (directly your open→in-progress gate pattern) — https://github.blog/ai-and-ml/generative-ai/agent-pull-requests-are-everywhere-heres-how-to-review-them/
- **Anthropic — "Demystifying evals for AI agents"** — pair deterministic checks (test suites) with model-based rubric graders — https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/demystifying-evals-for-ai-agents

**Plan critics / self-critique loops (papers):**
- **Reflexion** (Shinn et al.) — verbal self-reflection in an episodic memory buffer; **91% pass@1 on HumanEval vs GPT-4's 80%** (verified) — arXiv:2303.11366
- **Self-Refine** (Madaan et al.) — generate → self-feedback → refine — arXiv:2303.17651
- **CRITIC** (Gou et al., ICLR 2024) — tool-grounded critique; key caution: **LLMs cannot reliably self-correct *without external grounding*** — arXiv:2305.11738
- **Chain-of-Verification (CoVe)** — draft → plan verification questions → **answer them independently** → finalize; highly reusable for plan-checking — arXiv:2309.11495
- **LLM-Modulo** — LLM-generator + sound external verifier in a generate-test loop; **~4.6× plan-validity gain** on TravelPlanning where CoT/ReAct/Reflexion scored ~0% — strongest evidence a *separate verifier pass* dramatically improves plan quality — arXiv:2405.20625

**Reusable rubric/critic design (synthesized from Hamel Husain, Eugene Yan, Evidently, LLM-RUBRIC):**
- Prefer **binary pass/fail** over 1–5 scores (avoids criteria drift; forces an actionable decision). If nuance needed, max 3 levels.
- Emit a structured contract per dimension: `{ dimension, reasoning (CoT first), citation (to the specific text/criterion), verdict }`. CoT-before-verdict measurably improves accuracy. This mirrors rebar's existing completion-verifier contract (FAIL must cite the failing criterion + a source citation).
- **One dimension per evaluator call** (LLM-RUBRIC). For ticket/plan grading, reusable dimensions: AC present & testable; scope clarity; actionability (files/paths/steps named); verifiability (verify commands/tests); dependencies/risks identified; no internal contradiction. Aggregate **fail-closed** (overall PASS only if all critical dimensions pass).
- **Ground the critic** (CRITIC/CoVe): have it generate verification questions and answer them against the actual codebase/artifact rather than trusting unaided self-assessment.
- **Bias controls** (Zheng): swap order for pairwise; grade substance not length (verbosity); don't use the plan's own author model as sole judge (self-enhancement).
- **Validate** the judge against hand-labels with precision/recall or Cohen's κ — *not* raw agreement (Hamel Husain https://hamel.dev/blog/posts/llm-judge/, Eugene Yan https://eugeneyan.com/writing/llm-evaluators/).

---

## 5. Prior-art research as a planning step (RFC / design-doc practice)

**Rust RFC template — verified verbatim.** Section order: Summary → Motivation → Guide-level → Reference-level → Drawbacks → **Rationale and alternatives** → **Prior art** → Unresolved questions → Future possibilities. — https://github.com/rust-lang/rfcs/blob/master/0000-template.md

- **"Prior art"** prompting questions (verbatim): "Does this feature exist in other programming languages and what experience have their community had?" · "Is this done by some other community and what were their experiences with it?" · "What lessons can we learn from what other communities have done here?" · "Are there any published papers or great posts that discuss this?" (The template explicitly notes absence of prior art is acceptable, and precedent alone doesn't justify an RFC.)
- **"Rationale and alternatives"** (verbatim): "Why is this design the best in the space of possible designs?" · "What other designs have been considered and what is the rationale for not choosing them?" · "What is the impact of not doing this?" · "Could this be done in a library or macro instead?"

**When the heavyweight review is warranted vs skipped (Rust threshold, verbatim).** Required for "**substantial**" changes (semantic/syntactic language changes, removing features, large `std` additions). **Skipped** for: "rephrasing, reorganizing, refactoring… changing shape does not change meaning"; additions that "strictly improve objective, numerical quality criteria" (speedups, warning removal); dev-only invisible changes. — https://github.com/rust-lang/rfcs/blob/master/README.md

**Python PEPs.** PEP 1 (normative): Rationale must "describe alternate designs that were considered"; rejected ideas "should be recorded along with the reasoning… preventing people from bringing up the same rejected idea again." PEP 12 template includes **Rationale** (mandatory) and **Rejected Ideas** (suggested). — https://peps.python.org/pep-0001/ · https://peps.python.org/pep-0012/

**Google design docs.** Malte Ubl, "Design Docs at Google" — "Alternatives considered" is "one of the most important" sections; list alternatives + the trade-offs that justify the chosen design; **skip the doc** if the solution is obvious or it'd just be "an implementation manual." — https://www.industrialempathy.com/posts/design-docs-at-google/

**ADRs / MADR.** Nygard's original ADR (Context/Decision/Consequences) — https://cognitect.com/blog/2011/11/15/documenting-architecture-decisions. MADR 4.0.0 adds an explicit **"Considered Options"** list + **"Decision Outcome"** ("Chosen option … because … comes out best") + per-option "Good/Bad/Neutral, because…" — a forced trade-off comparison. — https://adr.github.io/madr/

**Synthesis for your gate:** all four ecosystems encode the same control — before adopting a novel mechanism you must (a) enumerate alternatives, (b) justify why the choice beats them on trade-offs, and (c) survey prior art — and all four **gate this behind a "substantial vs trivial" threshold** so small changes skip it. Your "plan proposes novel architecture not validated against prior art" check has direct precedent here, including the precedent for *only firing it on substantial work*.

---

## 6. Sizing / effort-estimation automation

**Story points & "too big = epic" (with thresholds).** Story points = effort + complexity + risk (Mountain Goat). Modified Fibonacci (1,2,3,5,8,13,20,40,100) — non-linear gaps deliberately force decomposition. Atlassian: estimates above **~16h / ~20 points** are "too hard to estimate confidently → break down"; an **epic = too big for one sprint, must decompose**; rule of thumb: work measured in *weeks* should be an epic. — https://www.mountaingoatsoftware.com/blog/what-are-story-points · https://www.atlassian.com/agile/project-management/epics

**Automated estimation from issue text (papers):**
- **Deep-SE** — Choetkiertikul et al., "A deep learning model for estimating story points" — LSTM + Recurrent Highway Network on **title + description only**; benchmark of **23,313 issues / 16 projects**; beats baselines on MAE and Standardized Accuracy (all verified). arXiv:1609.00489 (IEEE TSE 2019).
- **GPT2SP** — Fu & Tantithamthavorn — fine-tuned GPT-2; 6–47% within-project MAE improvement over prior DL (IEEE TSE 49(2)).
- **LLM story-point estimation** — Shetty et al. (verified, **submitted March 2026**): **zero-shot LLMs beat supervised DL trained on 80% of the data**, on the same 16 projects. arXiv:2603.06276.

**Tools that flag oversized/stale work (published heuristics):**
- **LinearB** — PRs under **~225 LOC** (alt: ~150), **max 4 WIP stories**, alert on PRs **idle >3 days**; "almost exponential" relationship between PR size and review time; small PRs picked up ~20× faster. — https://linearb.io/blog/team-working-agreements
- **Swarmia** — issue/PR cycle time + Working-Agreement size/WIP caps with Slack alerts.
- **Jellyfish** — "stories estimated above some maximum threshold need to be broken down."

**Simplest automatable size proxies from text (ranked):**
1. **Structural footprint** (files/components mentioned, subtasks, AC count) — *most reliable*: "Early-Stage Prediction of Review Effort in AI-Generated PRs" found structural metrics hit **AUC > 0.95 vs text/CodeBERT's 0.52** (arXiv:2601.00753). For rebar this maps to **counting `set_file_impact` entries and AC checklist items**.
2. **Conjunction count** ("and"/"or") — an academic atomicity check flags noun phrases containing "and"/"or" as non-atomic/too-big (arXiv:2301.10070).
3. **Description length alone is weak/inconsistent** — use only combined with structural signals (arXiv:2507.06704).

**Sources:** Mountain Goat (above); Atlassian Fibonacci/epics (above); Deep-SE arXiv:1609.00489; Shetty et al. arXiv:2603.06276; LinearB (above); structural-footprint arXiv:2601.00753; atomicity arXiv:2301.10070.

---

## Cross-cutting takeaways for your gate design

1. **Model it as advisory, not a hard block.** The single strongest signal across DoR and INVEST literature is that readiness checklists become anti-patterns when they harden into mandatory gates. An overridable gate (like rebar's `--force-close`) that *surfaces and cites* problems aligns with both the prior art and rebar's existing "closed-without-signature is the durable signal" philosophy.
2. **Cheap structural pre-filter, then LLM.** Run automatable signals first (AC-item count, file-impact count, conjunction/verb regex, child-coverage check) and reserve the billable LLM-judge pass for tickets that pass the cheap filter or warrant deeper review — exactly the "substantial vs trivial" threshold that Rust/Google use to decide when heavyweight review fires.
3. **Borrow the verified rubric-judge contract.** One scoped binary check per dimension (LLM-RUBRIC), CoT-before-verdict, fail-closed aggregation, and every FAIL citing the failing criterion + a source citation — which is precisely the contract rebar's completion-verifier already uses, so the readiness gate can reuse that machinery.
4. **Ground the critic** (CRITIC/CoVe/LLM-Modulo): for "inaccurate file references," "ignores consumers," and "child tickets don't cover the parent," have the critic generate verification questions and check them against the actual codebase and the ticket's children — unaided self-critique is the documented failure mode.
5. **Novel-architecture check has direct RFC precedent:** force "alternatives considered + prior art surveyed," but only on substantial work — the Rust/PEP/Google/MADR "Considered Options + Rationale" sections are a ready-made rubric.
